Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Seeks articulate guests with viewpoints that are not necessarily those of ""the establishment."" ""Look for how a particular issue will affect John and Jane Doe -- how unemployment in one part of America will ultimately affect John's job."" The best success comes from people who are familiar with the specific needs and problems of St. Louis and the metro area. He'd like to see more stories relating to politics and controversy and fewer product sales and health issue related pitches. Keep in mind ""our talk show is caller driven,"" he advises. Avoid sending ""product demo stuff,"" he requests. Peeve: disguising a puff piece as legitimate news. Prefers email. Best times to reach him are Tue.-Fri., 10 a.m.-3 p.m. No unsolicited faxes.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Mihill produces the CBS station's "Total Information AM" four hour morning show from 5 to 9 am Central time. "We do a lot of news-oriented interviews so PR Pros should provide a cell phone contact and be agreeable to last minute bookings. You'd be surprised how many radio interviews are scheduled 10 minutes before broadcast," he says. "We're always looking for big name interviews from news, sports entertainment and arts. Guests do not have to be from or in St Louis. Pitch me directly anywhere from two days to two weeks in advance. If it's not appropriate for the morning show, I'll forward to other producers," he promises. "Pitches should be one page with no attachments. The subject line should be direct. Its good to say something like `our guest will tell you why your bank is ripping you off,' or `top surgeon says most back surgeries are not needed.' Things like `this would be good for your show. This is a great guest, or 'you'd like this guest' is bad," he adds. Follow-up calls are OK.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Co-hosts "Morning Meeting." It covers "everything from politics to movies," Rose says. Send him "more 'lifestyle' stuff. Tell me about your client's other side. We can have fun with that and transition into your agenda." He requests that PR pros do not offer "your client's latest opinion on subject 'X.'" Contact by fax or email with a press release, press kit or product sample. Calls are best in the morning. Guests should be "in the mood to talk. Someone not afraid of opening up." His advice: "Know what I do. Know my show (at least a little). Don't offer me someone that doesn't fit what I do. It wastes your time and mine."

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Mihill produces the CBS station's "Total Information AM" four hour morning show from 5 to 9 am Central time. "We do a lot of news-oriented interviews so PR Pros should provide a cell phone contact and be agreeable to last minute bookings. You'd be surprised how many radio interviews are scheduled 10 minutes before broadcast," he says. "We're always looking for big name interviews from news, sports entertainment and arts. Guests do not have to be from or in St Louis. Pitch me directly anywhere from two days to two weeks in advance. If it's not appropriate for the morning show, I'll forward to other producers," he promises. "Pitches should be one page with no attachments. The subject line should be direct. Its good to say something like `our guest will tell you why your bank is ripping you off,' or `top surgeon says most back surgeries are not needed.' Things like `this would be good for your show. This is a great guest, or 'you'd like this guest' is bad," he adds. Follow-up calls are OK.

Contact Profile: Frank Cusumano joined the NewsChannel 5 sports team in April 1993 as a Sports Reporter. A native St. Louisian, Cusumano attended DeSmet High School where he was an all-city guard and a part of an undefeated (32-0) State Championship basketball team.  Cusumano received a Bachelor of Arts degree in communications from the University of Missouri St. Louis, where he played basketball for four years on a scholarship.  Cusumano and his wife Monique have three children Alex, Brooke, and Dominic. They reside in Creve Coeur.
